auth.welcomeBackBody: Sign in and take part in city projects and participation processes.
auth.loginSubtitle: Please enter your credentials to continue.
auth.joinBody: Create an account and become part of the community – help shape your city.
auth.registerSubtitle: Create a free account and get started right away.

home.metaDescription: Stay informed, join the discussion and help shape {city}.
home.heroBody: UrbanKIT makes visible what is happening in {city} – and offers the opportunity to get directly involved.
home.aboutBody: UrbanKIT is the digital tool for citizen participation in {city}. It shows what is being planned and provides space for questions, discussion and your own voice – to actively help shape decisions.
home.sectionsBody: UrbanKIT brings participation together in one place and separates it visibly by colour: making projects visible, enabling collaboration and opening up knowledge – a place to stay informed and to help shape projects.
home.areaProjectsBody: Discover ongoing and completed projects: what is being worked on, what has been decided and how participation makes a difference.
home.areaCollabBody: Take an active part in projects: vote, exchange ideas and develop solutions together – structured in one place.
home.areaBasicsBody: Understand how good participation works: methods, background and orientation for planning and implementation.
home.projectsBody: See what is currently being worked on in UrbanKIT. Every project is a place for information, discussion and real participation.
home.projectsEmpty: No public projects at the moment.
home.joinBody: UrbanKIT is free and open to everyone in {city}. No prior knowledge is needed – just interest. Sign up, follow projects, ask questions and contribute your own perspective.

footer.tagline: The digital platform for citizen participation. Help shape your city.

starten.metaDescription: Participation in three steps: create an account, find a project, help shape it.
starten.heroBody: Sign up. Discover projects. Help shape them. That's how participation works in {city}.
starten.step1Desc: Registration takes less than a minute. How visible you are stays up to you. With an account, projects can not only be viewed but actively shaped.
starten.step2Desc: Find projects that affect or interest you, with a quick overview of which city projects are currently active – from traffic routing to green spaces. Every project shows what is being decided and where participation is possible.
starten.step3Desc: Within a project, the outcome takes shape together with the city and other citizens. Contribute your own perspective, give feedback and follow how the input feeds into the further process.
starten.featAnonBody: You can choose a username that has nothing to do with your real name. Only a valid email address is required – and it is never shown publicly.
starten.featAppmoBody: An existing account with AppmoId, the city's citizen portal, is enough to sign in to UrbanKIT – no second account needed.
starten.featDeleteBody: The account can be deleted completely at any time. All personal data is then removed irreversibly.
starten.featSearchBody: Filters by topic, phase or district lead quickly to what fits: a street, a neighbourhood, specific topics.
starten.featContextBody: Every project explains the context: what is being planned here? What has been decided so far? Which options are on the table?
starten.featJoinBody: There are several ways to join a project: directly on the platform, at a public event or via an invitation code.
starten.featModulesBody: Every project uses building blocks from Urban KIT – structured methods and tools that make participation tangible: from polls to collaborative maps.
starten.featBasicsBody: UrbanKIT makes planning knowledge accessible: background on urban-planning methods and insights from completed projects are available to use.
starten.featResultBody: The result is something concrete – carried jointly by the city, experts and citizens. Every contribution is documented and feeds directly into the planning.
starten.invBody: Staff of the city of {city} can invite citizens to participate directly. The code is in the invitation sent by post or email.
starten.invHint: It takes you straight to the relevant project – with no further hurdles.
starten.ctaBody: Sign up now and get started right away – in less than a minute.

about.metaTitle: UrbanKIT explained – making urban development transparent
about.metaDescription: What is UrbanKIT? How does the platform work? Everything about the digital tool for citizen participation.
about.heroBody: UrbanKIT is the digital tool for citizen participation in {city}. Transparent, accessible and open to everyone – a place to follow urban development, understand it and get actively involved.
about.bgP1: UrbanKIT is the digital participation tool developed as part of the project SC PUT – Smart City Participation and Usability Toolbox by a team from the Institute for Design Strategies in collaboration with the city of {city}. The aim is to make urban decisions visible, understandable and open to participation – for everyone who lives, works or is active in {city}.
about.bgP2: UrbanKIT shows which projects are currently running, which decisions have been made and where participation is possible. No prior knowledge is needed: the platform explains contexts, provides background and supports having a say before decisions are made.
about.bgP3: UrbanKIT combines information, exchange and participation in one place. How actively you take part is entirely up to you.
about.sectionsBody: UrbanKIT is divided into three areas that offer different points of entry but work towards one shared goal: making urban development understandable and accessible.
about.areaProjectsBody: View ongoing and completed projects and understand what is being decided, who is involved and what the current status is.
about.areaCollabBody: The heart of participation. This is where people who want to actively shape the city come together – working on projects, discussing, exchanging ideas, voting and developing ideas together.
about.areaBasicsBody: Understand how participation works. Build knowledge, find methods, justify participation – with background and context for everyone who wants to understand participatory processes.
about.ctaBody: Register for free and get going – discover projects, stay informed and get actively involved.

kontakt.metaDescription: Get in touch with the Urban KIT platform.
kontakt.heroBody: Questions about the platform, about a project, or feedback to share? We look forward to your message.
kontakt.supportHint: For technical problems with your account, please contact <mail>{email}</mail>.
kontakt.sentBody: We will get back to you as soon as possible.

projekteArchiv.metaDescription: An overview of all ongoing and completed urban development projects.
projekteArchiv.heroBody: Ongoing and completed city projects – documented and transparent: planned initiatives, those involved and the current status at a glance.
projekteArchiv.introP1: This is where what is planned, decided and implemented in {city} becomes visible. Every project is documented – from the current status to the decision.
projekteArchiv.introP2: Searching specifically by topic or location shows what is changing in your own surroundings. New developments, decisions and documents are added continuously.
projekteArchiv.featProjectsBody: What is planned, what has been decided and how projects develop – from the first draft to completion.
projekteArchiv.featFilterBody: Quickly find what is relevant – in your own neighbourhood or on a specific topic.
projekteArchiv.featArchiveBody: Completed projects stay visible too – as a basis for later decisions and as a record for citizens.
projekteArchiv.activeBody: See what is currently being worked on in UrbanKIT. Every project is a place for information, discussion and real participation.
projekteArchiv.empty: No public projects at the moment.
projekteArchiv.archiveBody: These projects are completed. All information and results remain accessible.

alleProjekte.metaDescription: An overview of all public urban development projects.
alleProjekte.heroP1: Here projects can not only be followed but used as a source of knowledge. Every project stays documented – with decisions, methods and results.
alleProjekte.heroP2: Over time, this builds a knowledge base. The archive makes visible how urban development actually works and brings together experience that can be used for future projects.
alleProjekte.resultsFound: {count, plural, one {# project found} other {# projects found}}

zusammenarbeit.metaDescription: The digital project space for teams: modules for news, polls, tasks and more.
zusammenarbeit.heroBody: The heart of participation. This is where people who want to actively shape the city come together – working on projects, discussing, exchanging ideas, voting and developing ideas. Citizens, administration and civic society interact directly here.
zusammenarbeit.roomP1: Every project on UrbanKIT gets its own project space – a shared workspace where everyone involved comes together: administration staff, invited citizens, civic society.
zusammenarbeit.roomP2: Which modules are active depends on the project. Public content is visible to everyone – internal work areas remain reserved for the team.
zusammenarbeit.modulesBody: Modules are the building blocks of the project space. Each module serves a clear purpose and can be activated per project. That is what makes structured participation possible.

module.metaDescription: All modules of the digital project space: news, polls, collab board and more.
module.heroBody: Every project space is made up of modules. The team decides which ones are active – keeping the space clear and focused on the essentials.
module.modNewsDesc: Project updates and announcements for everyone involved. Public or team-only.
module.modPollDesc: Structured voting – anonymous or with an account. Results visible transparently.
module.modBoardDesc: A shared whiteboard for visual collaboration in real time.
module.modTasksDesc: Task lists with assignment, status and due date for the whole team.
module.modCalendarDesc: Appointments, workshops and events at a glance – for everyone involved.
module.modForumDesc: A discussion area for substantive questions, ideas and structured feedback.
module.modFilesDesc: Central storage for documents, plans and materials – versioned and searchable.
module.modAgentDesc: An AI assistant with access to the project context. Summarises, answers questions.
module.modChatDesc: Direct communication within the team and via direct message – integrated into the project space.

grundlagen.metaDescription: Understand how participation works. Build knowledge. Find methods.
grundlagen.heroBody: Understand how participation works. Build knowledge. Find methods. Justify participation. With background, methods and context for city staff and everyone who wants to understand how participatory processes work.
grundlagen.basicsBody: The basics form the backbone – understanding participation, shaping projects, knowing the legal framework. And anyone who wants to get started straight away will find the right methods further down.
grundlagen.cardPartText: What participation means, how it works and why it is decisive in urban development.
grundlagen.cardPlanText: From the first idea to implementation – a guide through the phases of a participatory city project.
grundlagen.cardLawText: Laws, participation rights and the regulatory basis of the platform – explained clearly.
grundlagen.methodsBody: Methods are the tools of participation. They describe concretely how processes are designed, people are involved and results are secured. Every method has emerged from real projects and provides information on its goal, effort and purpose.

partizipation.metaDescription: What is participation, why does it matter and how does it succeed in urban development?
partizipation.heroBody: What we mean by participation, why it matters and how it succeeds in practice – from definition to evaluation.
partizipation.s1Body:
For us, participation means involving the interests and needs of those affected in decision-making processes early and transparently. People actively take part in the processes, decisions, systems and developments that affect them.

It is not a means of retroactively legitimising decisions that have already been made. It is not only a democratic principle but a deliberately designed process and lived practice.

When participation succeeds, those involved experience that their own voice counts, that decisions become comprehensible, that trust grows and that a sense of cohesion can emerge.

Projects gain quality as a result, because they are closer to people's actual needs and less prone to conflict or blockages.

What is decisive is that roles, scope, goals and decisions are named transparently. Participation only works when it is taken seriously, implemented in a structured way and visibly embedded in decision-making. Participation is only credible when it is clear what influence contributions actually have.
partizipation.s2Body: Participation increases acceptance of planning decisions, brings local knowledge into the process and strengthens trust between administration and the public. [Content to follow]
partizipation.s3Body: Formal participation procedures are legally anchored in Germany – among others in the Federal Building Code (BauGB) and in the municipal codes of the federal states. [Content to follow]
partizipation.s6Title: The key questions for every participation project
partizipation.s11Title: Participation in the smart city context

recht.metaDescription: Legal foundations and requirements for municipal citizen participation in Germany.
recht.heroBody: Which laws apply, what municipalities are obliged to do and how participation is designed to be legally sound.
recht.s1Body:
The Federal Building Code (BauGB) is the most important legal basis for formal citizen participation in urban planning. It requires that citizens be involved early and comprehensively in land-use planning procedures.

Early public participation (§ 3 para. 1 BauGB) should take place before a development plan or land-use plan is adopted. The aim is to inform the public about the general goals and purposes of the planning and to give it the opportunity to comment and discuss.

The formal public display (§ 3 para. 2 BauGB) obliges municipalities to display draft plans publicly for at least 30 days and to accept objections.

All submissions received must be reviewed and the outcome communicated to those who raised them. Objections that are not taken into account must be justified.
recht.s2Title: Municipal codes of the federal states
recht.s2Body:
In addition to the BauGB, the municipal codes of the 16 federal states regulate citizen participation at the local level. They differ considerably – from simple information obligations to far-reaching participation rights.

Typical instruments in the municipal codes are:

  • Citizens' petitions & referendums: Direct democracy at the local level – citizens can force decisions on specific matters.
  • Residents' motion: The right to put certain topics on the agenda of the municipal council.
  • Residents' question time: Public questioning of municipal council members.
  • Information rights: The right to information about municipal affairs.

Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg and North Rhine-Westphalia have particularly detailed regulations. It is advisable to check the specific municipal code of the respective federal state.
recht.s3Body:
Informal participation procedures are not required by law but are recognised politically and professionally. They complement the formal procedures and allow for more creative, lower-threshold formats.

Typical informal formats are future workshops, planning cells, citizens' forums, online consultations or neighbourhood talks. They can be designed flexibly – but must be communicated transparently and clearly distinguished from formal procedures.

Important: informal participation does not replace formal procedures. It can prepare, accompany or complement them – but not replace them.

The results of informal procedures have no binding legal effect. It is all the more important to communicate transparently what weight the results carry in the overall process.
recht.s4Body:
Participation procedures often collect personal data – names, email addresses, location data or expressions of opinion. The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) set out a clear framework for this.

Key obligations in participation formats:

  • Clarify the legal basis: Data collection needs a basis – usually consent or public interest.
  • Provide a privacy policy: Transparency about purpose, storage period and sharing.
  • Data minimisation: Collect only as much data as necessary.
  • Observe deletion periods: Delete data once the purpose has been fulfilled.

For digital participation platforms, data protection compliance should be checked before launch. A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) may be necessary for high-risk procedures.
recht.s5Body:
Genuine participation requires accessibility. Several laws oblige public bodies to remove barriers:

  • Disability Equality Act (BGG): Accessible communication and accessibility of public services.
  • State equality acts: Gender equity in planning and participation.
  • WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines): The standard for accessible digital services – mandatory for public bodies from WCAG 2.1 AA.

In practice this means: events in accessible spaces, multilingual materials, plain language, sign language interpreters where needed and technically accessible online tools.

Accessibility is not an add-on – it is a basic prerequisite for participation that reaches everyone.
recht.s6Title: Liability and binding force of participation results
recht.s6Body:
An often underestimated legal question: what binding force do participation results have? The answer is clear – in principle none. Participation results are politically significant but not legally binding.

Decisions still rest with the democratically elected bodies. No participation format can replace or limit their decision-making authority.

This creates responsibility for communication: if participation results are not implemented, or only partially, this must be justified comprehensibly – otherwise there is a risk of losing trust.

Participation becomes relevant in liability terms above all in the case of procedural errors in the formal area: if legally required participation steps are omitted, this can render planning decisions void.
recht.s7Body:
A practical orientation for preparing participation procedures:

  • Formal obligations checked? Check the BauGB, municipal code and sector-specific laws.
  • Data protection clarified? Legal basis, privacy policy, deletion concept.
  • Accessibility ensured? Venue, language, digital tools.
  • Binding force communicated transparently? What happens with the results?
  • Documentation planned? Evidence of participation for later procedural steps.
  • Information on legal remedies? For formal procedures: note the deadlines for objections.

projektplanung.metaDescription: From the first idea to implementation – a guide through the phases of a participatory city project.
projektplanung.heroBody: From the first idea to implementation – a guide through the phases of a participatory city project.
projektplanung.s0Ziel: Create a basic understanding and motivation: why are we doing participation at all? Which values, attitudes and guidelines carry us? Only when everyone is clear about why participation matters do trust, openness and energy emerge for the whole process.
projektplanung.s0Intro: Suppose there is a good idea — a new app, a square redesign, a mobility strategy. Even before the question “What exactly do we want to change?” comes the question: “Why do we want to involve people?” Is it just obligation, or a genuine will to co-create? A clearly formulated “why” is the starting signal that motivates people to get involved in the first place.

s0Todos.text: Why participation? Obligation, image, quality, legitimacy, innovation?
Openness: which questions are genuinely open?
How do we handle the results?
Which voices do we absolutely want to hear?
Resources (time, budget, staff) and limits (political, legal, organisational)
A shared sentence that carries everyone: “We do participation because …”

projektplanung.s0Wichtig: Without an honest “why”, there is a risk of token participation. A shared foundation of values protects against conflicts in later steps. Attitude shapes the whole process — more than any tool.

s0Methoden.name: Kick-off dialogue
s0Methoden.description: Internal team conversation · gather vision & values

projektplanung.s1Ziel: Get to know the current situation, the stakeholders and the subject area.
projektplanung.s1Intro: Before planning, there must be understanding — who is affected, what already exists, what is expected?

s1Todos.text: Who is affected, who has influence, who should be involved?
Research existing plans, decisions and reports

projektplanung.s1Wichtig: Without solid familiarisation, blind spots emerge that cost trust later.
projektplanung.s2Ziel: Develop a coherent concept that defines goals, target groups, formats and timeline.
projektplanung.s2Intro: The concept is the roadmap. It answers: whom do we involve, with what, when and how?

s2Todos.text: Whom do we want to reach, whom must we reach?
Online, analogue, hybrid — what fits the context?
Account for milestones, buffers and political dates

projektplanung.s2Wichtig: A concept without resource planning is a wish list. Stay realistic.
projektplanung.s3Ziel: Clarify the operational level: who does what by when, with which resources?
projektplanung.s3Intro: Now the concept becomes a concrete action plan with responsibilities and budgets.

s3Todos.text: Costs for venues, materials, communication, staff
How do we inform the public about the participation?

projektplanung.s3Wichtig: Without clear responsibilities, everything ends up resting on one person.
projektplanung.s4Ziel: Implement and document the planned participation formats.
projektplanung.s4Intro: Now the actual participation takes place — events, workshops, surveys.
projektplanung.s4Wichtig: Documentation is not an afterthought — it is part of the process and builds trust.

s4Methoden.name: World Café
s4Methoden.description: Group format for broad topic exploration

s4Methoden.name: Online survey
s4Methoden.description: Digital participation for broad target groups

projektplanung.s5Ziel: Reflect on the ongoing process and adjust where necessary.
projektplanung.s5Intro: Participation is not a linear process. Regular reflection helps to stay on course.

s5Todos.text: What is going well, what is not?

projektplanung.s5Wichtig: Pursuing rigid plans in dynamic contexts costs more than reacting flexibly.
projektplanung.s6Ziel: Conclude the participation with dignity, hand over results and capture what was learned.
projektplanung.s6Intro: The conclusion is just as important as the start. Participants want to know what became of their contributions.

s6Todos.text: What was heard, what will be implemented, what not (and why)?
What did we learn for next time?

projektplanung.s6Wichtig: A missing conclusion destroys trust. People want to know what happened to their contributions.

projectDetail.metaFallbackDesc: Find out about the {title} project and get actively involved.
projectDetail.aboutEmpty: There is no detailed description for this project yet.
projectDetail.participationBody: Every project goes through seven phases – from the first idea to completion. This shows where the project currently stands and how to get involved.
projectDetail.joinBody: Questions or interested in taking an active part? The contacts are happy to help – or become part of the project directly, free and open to everyone.

newsDetail.empty: This post has no content yet.
